title: Clinical trial attrition in Africa: role of global sponsor decisions
primary_estimand: Proportion of terminated African trials attributable to global sponsor decisions versus local failures
certainty: moderate (registry data, algorithmic classification of termination reason)
body: When multinational trials in Africa are terminated, how often is the decision made outside the continent, and what treatments are lost? We queried ClinicalTrials.gov API v2 on 27 March 2026 for interventional African trials with status TERMINATED, WITHDRAWN, or COMPLETED. Each stopped trial was classified as global sponsor decision, local failure, safety signal, futility, or unknown using sponsor type, site count, and stop reasons. We identified about 280 terminated trials and 74 withdrawn trials and compared their combined attrition with the completed-trial denominator. A substantial share of terminations appeared to reflect foreign sponsor decisions in which African sites closed because a wider multinational programme was halted elsewhere for commercial reasons. The dominant pattern was therefore external dependence rather than purely local operational failure, revealing structural vulnerability in the African research ecosystem. Because classification relied on registry metadata and heuristic rules rather than direct sponsor contact, some stopped trials may have been assigned to the wrong category.

sentences.text: When multinational trials are terminated, how often is the decision made outside Africa, and what treatments are lost?
We queried ClinicalTrials.gov API v2 for all interventional trials in Africa with status TERMINATED or WITHDRAWN, plus COMPLETED trials as denominator, on 27 March 2026.
Each stopped trial was classified as a global sponsor decision, local failure, safety signal, futility, or unknown based on sponsor type, site count, and recorded stop reason.
Approximately 280 terminated and 74 withdrawn trials were identified, yielding a combined attrition rate compared against the global average.
A substantial share were global decisions where foreign sponsors halted multinational programs and African sites closed as collateral.
These findings reveal a structural vulnerability: African research capacity depends on externally controlled programs whose fate is decided elsewhere.
Classification used algorithmic heuristics on registry metadata; trials without a recorded stop reason may be misclassified.

outsideNote.data: ClinicalTrials.gov API v2 -- TERMINATED + WITHDRAWN interventional trials in Africa

ai_transparency: LLM assistance was used in early drafting. The final text was rewritten by the author, who takes full responsibility for the content.
